unit.name: Implementation consistency, contamination, and keeping test sets alive
unit.description: Explains why benchmark results can change with formatting and implementation details, then discusses contamination, overfitting, and mitigations such as private or dynamic test sets.
unit.summary: This segment argues that even apparently simple benchmarks can be unstable. For multiple-choice tasks, changing how answers are formatted can change generations and model rankings, and MMLU historically had multiple incompatible implementations that differed in prompting and answer-selection procedures such as constrained decoding versus token or log-likelihood scoring [ts=3840s] [ts=3878s] [ts=3916s]. Yann then moves to contamination: benchmark scores can be inflated when test data or close variants appear in pretraining, illustrated by code-benchmark anecdotes where GPT-4 did very well on older Codeforces problems but failed on newer ones [ts=4032s] [ts=4063s]. The lecture separates contamination from broader overfitting to public test sets, then suggests mitigations including private or regenerated test sets such as GSM1K, dynamic test sets such as Dynabench, and leakage detection heuristics such as unusually confident answers or sensitivity to the original order of examples [ts=4153s] [ts=4197s] [ts=4223s].

key_points.text: Multiple-choice rankings can change if you relabel the answer options or alter the exact formatting.
MMLU once had multiple mainstream implementations that produced different scores because prompting and scoring conventions differed.
Constrained decoding, raw-token prediction, and likelihood-based scoring can yield substantially different benchmark results.
A sharp performance drop from old to new benchmark items can indicate contamination from pretraining on the benchmark data.
Closed models and massive pretraining corpora make contamination hard to verify directly.
Rapid achievement of 'human-level' scores on public benchmarks may reflect contamination or repeated tuning on the test set.
Mitigations include private or regenerated test sets like GSM1K and dynamic benchmarks like Dynabench.
Possible leakage checks include looking for overconfident answer probabilities or drops when example order is scrambled.

For multiple-choice tasks, changing how answers are formatted can change generations and model rankings, and MMLU historically had multiple incompatible implementations that differed in prompting and answer-selection procedures such as constrained decoding versus token or log-likelihood scoring [ts=3840s] [ts=3878s] [ts=3916s]. Yann then moves to contamination: benchmark scores can be inflated when test data or close variants appear in pretraining, illustrated by code-benchmark anecdotes where GPT-4 did very well on older Codeforces problems but failed on newer ones [ts=4032s] [ts=4063s]. The lecture separates contamination from broader overfitting to public test sets, then suggests mitigations including private or regenerated test sets such as GSM1K, dynamic test sets such as Dynabench, and leakage detection heuristics such as unusually confident answers or sensitivity to the original order of examples [ts=4153s] [ts=4197s] [ts=4223s].

kp_catalog.name: Benchmark contamination and overfitting
kp_catalog.description: Benchmark scores can shift because of formatting, implementation differences, train-test leakage, or repeated tuning on public tests. The problem that benchmark examples may leak into pretraining data, undermining claims of true generalization.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because benchmark contamination and overfitting clarifies benchmark scores can shift because of formatting, implementation differences, train-test leakage, or repeated tuning on public tests, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices though importance labels split between critical and high.

kp_catalog.name: Current LLM benchmark suites and task coverage
kp_catalog.description: Modern LLM evaluation uses broad benchmark suites spanning math, knowledge, code, law, medicine, and other domains.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because current LLM benchmark suites and task coverage clarifies modern LLM evaluation uses broad benchmark suites spanning math, knowledge, code, law, medicine, and other domains, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
